svg.title: Recruitment agent system — plain-English overview of what's running in production
svg.desc: Orchestrator (left band, teal) runs each stage in order. Pipeline stages 0-6 flow vertically in the middle. Graphiti (right band, purple) is the shared memory every stage reads from and writes to. Nightly learning loop at the bottom.
svg.text: Recruitment agent — what's actually running in production
how each stage works, in plain English. teal = LLM agents · orange = rule-based or human input · grey = action · purple = shared memory

g.text: STAGE 0 · RECRUITER WRITES DOWN THE ROLE INTENT (HUMAN)
The recruiter writes down what they're really looking for — the things the job spec misses.
"Need ambiguity tolerance." "Won't work for someone who can't push back on the CFO."
"This is a burnout backfill — resilience matters more than depth."
Versioned, so old decisions can show the intent that was active when they were made.
STAGE 1 · THREE QUICK SCORERS RUN IN PARALLEL
STAGE 2 · SHOULD WE SEND THIS CANDIDATE A TASK?
Looks at what the candidate already has (GitHub, portfolio, prior assessments). Picks one of three:
— Send a task. Picks the template with the best track record for this role family.
— Skip the task. Their existing artifacts already cover what we'd test.
— Ask for artifacts. No good template exists yet — better to admit unknown than guess.
STAGE 3 · GRADING THE TASK SUBMISSION (ASYNC — IF SENT)
When the candidate submits the task, this grades it against the role's rubric.
Returns a score per rubric criterion plus how confident it is. Runs whenever submissions arrive,
STAGE 4 · COMBINE THE SCORES INTO ONE DECISION
Takes all the scores (pre-screen, CV, graph priors, assessment) plus what's worked at this
role historically and outputs one of four recommendations:
Advance · Reject · Do nothing · Escalate (when the scorers disagree or are too uncertain).
Simple statistical model — appropriate for current scale. Will grow more sophisticated later.
Approve — the recommendation fires the action.
Override — recruiter does something different manually; the system logs why.
Teach — sends the decision back to pending with a structured correction (which sub-agent
got it wrong, was the score too high or too low, was this a one-off or a pattern).
Whatever advances or rejects a candidate goes through the same functions whether
triggered by the recruiter directly or by an approval. One audit trail for both paths.
Same idempotency, same logging, same authorisation. The recruiter is always actor of record.
Reached interview · Got an offer · Hired · Rejected later · Withdrew —
each lands in the graph linked to the original decision. This is how the system learns.
NIGHTLY · THE LEARNING LOOP (READS FROM GRAPHITI)
